question: The 2026 stock split was completed shortly before filing. What are the typical underwriting and market-structure rationales for such a split, and how does the filing reflect the split in historical per-share data?

A forward split reduces the per-share price by the split ratio, making individual shares more affordable to retail investors.
The SpaceX prospectus provides for a retail investor allocation through online brokerage platforms at the same IPO price as institutional allocations.
A lower post-split price broadens the addressable retail order book.
U.S. exchanges traditionally quote and trade in round lots of 100 shares.
A 5-for-1 split multiplies the float fivefold.
More shares outstanding at a lower price typically supports tighter bid-ask spreads, greater depth at each price level, and higher share-volume turnover.
Underwriters generally prefer an offering price within a range that is familiar to the market and compatible with exchange listing standards.
A lower per-share price permits finer-grained option strike prices and RSU grant sizing.
Certain index inclusion methodologies and exchange listing criteria reference per-share price thresholds.
The filing applies retrospective retroactive adjustment to all historical per-share data.
All share and per share information included in the prospectus has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented, unless explicitly noted otherwise.

question: When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?
facts: The filing does not explicitly resolve whether future orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed.

Capitalized costs for existing satellites include build costs plus capitalized launch costs.
Useful lives are 5 years for broadband satellites and 3 years for first-generation mobile satellites.
Depreciation for existing satellites is straight-line over estimated useful lives.
AI infrastructure and data centers are classified as PP&E.
Useful lives for AI infrastructure are 5–6 years for servers and networking equipment and 20–25 years for data center infrastructure.
Interest is capitalized during construction for significant long-term projects such as AI infrastructure data centers.
The majority of Starship costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred because Starship is in the development stage.
Once Starship reaches commercialization, costs generally will be capitalized rather than expensed as R&D.
Maintenance and repairs are expensed as incurred.
Refurbishment and enhancements that extend life or functionality are capitalized and depreciated ratably.
By inference from disclosed precedents, development-stage costs for orbital AI compute satellites would likely be expensed to R&D.
By inference from disclosed precedents, post-deployment operational assets for orbital AI compute satellites would likely be capitalized as PP&E.

question: What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.
facts: The filing states Starship is expected to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026.
The filing states Starship payload delivery is expected following additional flight tests.
The filing states deployment of next-generation V3 satellites is expected to begin in the second half of 2026.
The filing states V3 satellites are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite.
The filing states a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO.
The filing states deploying 60 V3 satellites per launch represents a twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch.
The filing states the company plans to deploy next-generation V2 Mobile satellites in 2027.
The filing states V2 Mobile satellites are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to the first-generation constellation.
The filing states Starship is anticipated to be able to deploy approximately 50 mobile satellites per launch.
The filing states Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites.
The filing states the Spectrum Acquisition Closing is expected to occur on or about November 30, 2027.
The filing states the relevant 5G NR-NTN bands are not currently supported by RF front-end hardware for the provision of 5G-like service in any commercially available mobile devices.
The filing states delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future.
The filing states there can be no assurance that the company will be able to achieve its objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all.
The filing states there can be no assurance that conditions for the Spectrum Acquisition Closing will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all.
The filing states there can be no assurance that handset hardware modifications for 5G NR-NTN compliance will be adopted on the company's preferred timeline, or at all.
The filing uses hedged, forward-looking verbs — 'expect,' 'plan,' 'anticipate' — rather than definitive commitments for all three programs.
The V2 Mobile language ('plan to expand... by deploying') is the softest of the three programs, while Starship and V3 use the slightly firmer 'expect to commence/begin.'
Starship and V3 share an identical 2H 2026 target with no disclosed buffer.
V3 deployment inherits 100% of Starship's schedule risk with zero disclosed fallback path.

V2 Mobile carries the highest aggregate timing risk because it stacks five independent gating conditions: (1) Starship operational, (2) EchoStar spectrum closing by ~Nov 30, 2027, (3) U.S. and international regulatory approvals, (4) MNO partnerships, and (5) handset RF front-end modifications.
The EchoStar closing date of November 30, 2027 leaves essentially no 2027 commercial-service runway even if all other conditions are met on time.
The filing's language suggests the disclosed timelines are aspirational rather than committed.
The filing's language suggests slippage of any one program — particularly Starship — would cascade into V3 and V2 Mobile.
The wording suggests elevated, cascading timing risk across all three programs, with risk increasing from Starship → V3 → V2 Mobile.

question: The glossary defines Terafab as a chip manufacturing initiative targeting one terawatt of compute *capacity* annually. Does the filing disclose (i) a target completion date or phased timeline, (ii) any capital commitment figure or funding source, (iii) expected unit economics or cost per watt, or (iv) the organizational entity responsible for execution? For each of the four items, state explicitly whether it is disclosed, partially disclosed, or absent, and explain what the absence of each item prevents an analyst from modeling.
facts: The S-1 glossary defines Terafab as a chip manufacturing initiative with a long-term goal of producing one terawatt of compute capacity annually.
The filing describes Terafab as expected to be the world's largest chip manufacturing facility.
The strategy for Terafab is to vertically integrate across the design of lithography masks, fabrication of logic and memory chips, and design of advanced packaging in a single closed-loop plant.
Terafab is described as a collaboration with Tesla (framework announced March 2026) and Intel (joined April 2026).
Terafab supports two chip types: terrestrial edge/inference chips for Tesla's Optimus robots and vehicles, and space-environment chips for SpaceX's orbital compute infrastructure.
Disclosure of a target completion date or phased timeline for Terafab is absent.
The filing states that any specific development timelines, milestones, and capital expenditures for Terafab projects have not yet been determined.
The absence of a target completion date or phased timeline prevents an analyst from phasing capital expenditure deployment across years.
The absence of a target completion date or phased timeline prevents an analyst from scheduling when the one-terawatt capacity would come online for DCF purposes.
Disclosure of a capital commitment figure or funding source for Terafab is absent.
SpaceX expects to increase capital expenditures substantially in the future in connection with programs including Terafab.

The absence of a capital commitment figure or funding source prevents an analyst from sizing the funding requirement or assessing whether SpaceX's balance sheet can absorb it without dilutive equity raises or incremental debt.
The absence of a capital commitment figure or funding source prevents an analyst from estimating return on invested capital or payback period.
Disclosure of expected unit economics or cost per watt for Terafab is absent.

The filing provides qualitative claims that vertical integration will create speed and cost advantages and reduce costs, but provides no cost-per-watt, cost-per-chip, yield assumptions, wafer-pricing, or gross-margin targets.
The absence of expected unit economics or cost per watt prevents an analyst from quantifying cost savings that would flow to SpaceX's compute cost line.
The absence of expected unit economics or cost per watt prevents an analyst from benchmarking Terafab's prospective cost-per-watt against third-party suppliers.
Disclosure of the organizational entity responsible for execution is partially disclosed.
SpaceX refers to Terafab as its initiative intended to alleviate potential future chip shortages at SpaceX.
SpaceX agreed with Tesla on a general framework for the future development of Terafab.
Intel is expected to contribute its expertise in designing, fabricating, and packaging ultra-high performance chips to help Terafab scale.
Neither Tesla nor Intel are obligated to remain a part of the Terafab project, and SpaceX may not enter into any definitive agreements with them.

question: The filing discloses two distinct voting mechanisms: (i) Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and (ii) combined Class A and Class B voting power determining the remaining board seats and general shareholder matters. Explain precisely how this dual-mechanism structure differs economically and practically from a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure in which all directors are elected by the combined class vote. What is the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest?
facts: In a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure, Class B's control of the board is contingent and proportional, depending on Class B maintaining enough economic ownership relative to Class A so that 10× its stake exceeds Class A's stake.
In SpaceX's dual-mechanism structure, Class B voting separately as a class elects 51% of the total authorized directors for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding.
In SpaceX's dual-mechanism structure, Class A and Class B voting together as a single class elect the remaining directors and decide all general shareholder matters.
Class B Directors may be removed only by a majority of Class B voting separately as a class.
Vacancies in Class B seats may be filled only by Class B or the remaining Class B Directors, and not any other persons.
In a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure, Class A can mathematically overcome Class B's 10× multiplier if Class A accumulates more than approximately 90.9% of the economic interest.
In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is not a function of relative economic ownership.
So long as a single Class B share remains outstanding, Class B elects 51% of the board regardless of whether Class A holds 10%, 50%, 90%, or 99.99% of the economic interest.
There is no economic threshold Class A can cross to capture the Class B Director seats.
In a conventional structure, issuing additional Class A shares dilutes Class B's voting percentage and can eventually erode its board control.
In SpaceX's structure, the 51% Class B Director block is immune to dilution.
In SpaceX's structure, equity issuances of Class A or Class C cannot reduce Class B below 51% of the board so long as any Class B share is outstanding.
In SpaceX's design, the 10-vote-per-share feature does the work only for electing the 49% Common Stock Directors, general shareholder matters, and removal of Common Stock Directors.
For the 51% Class B Director block, the 10× feature is essentially redundant; the separate-class election is what delivers control.
SpaceX's structure is a stronger entrenchment device than a pure high-vote dual class because it layers an absolute class-vote lock on top of the proportional 10× multiplier.

Even if Class A shareholders collectively held a majority or supermajority of the economic interest in SpaceX, their ability to influence board composition would be structurally capped at the 49% Common Stock Director block.
Class A cannot elect, remove, or replace any Class B Director under any circumstance while Class B shares remain outstanding.
Class A's maximum achievable board representation is the Common Stock Directors, which is 49% of the board rounded down.
A Class A economic majority cannot translate into board control; the economic interest and board control are decoupled for the majority of seats.
No amount of Class A accumulation changes the 51% Class B block.
The only route to influencing Class B Director seats is conversion or elimination of Class B shares, which requires Class B's separate-class consent.
Class A cannot remove a Class B Director even with unanimous Class A support; only Class B, voting separately as a class, can do so.
Class B participates with 10× weighting in the combined-class vote to remove Common Stock Directors.
Class A's influence over its own 49% block is subject to Class B's 10× voting power on the combined-class vote.
A Class A shareholder with a majority of economic interest is a permanent minority in board-composition terms.
Economic ownership confers no corresponding ability to determine or influence through ordinary voting the majority of the board.
SpaceX's structure converts what would otherwise be a proportional, dilutable, threshold-dependent founder lock into an absolute, dilution-proof, threshold-free lock on a board majority.
For a Class A shareholder, holding a majority of the economic interest is economically real but governance-irrelevant with respect to the majority of the board.
The separate-class election of the Class B Directors is the dispositive mechanism for board control, not the 10-vote multiplier.
